title: SQL Cheat Sheet — Complete Syntax Reference

sections.heading: DDL — Data Definition Language
sections.content:
-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
-- CREATE TABLE
-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
CREATE TABLE delivery (
    id              BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    tracking_code   VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    status          VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL DEFAULT 'PLANNED',
    driver_id       BIGINT REFERENCES driver(id),
    route_id        BIGINT,
    scheduled_at    TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL,
    delivered_at    TIMESTAMPTZ,
    notes           TEXT,
    weight_kg       NUMERIC(8,2),
    is_express      BOOLEAN DEFAULT false,
    created_at      TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    updated_at      TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    CONSTRAINT fk_route FOREIGN KEY (route_id) REFERENCES route(id),
    CONSTRAINT chk_weight CHECK (weight_kg > 0)
);

-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
-- ALTER TABLE
-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
ALTER TABLE delivery ADD COLUMN priority INT DEFAULT 0;
ALTER TABLE delivery DROP COLUMN notes;
ALTER TABLE delivery ALTER COLUMN status SET DEFAULT 'DRAFT';
ALTER TABLE delivery ALTER COLUMN status TYPE VARCHAR(30);
ALTER TABLE delivery RENAME COLUMN is_express TO express;
ALTER TABLE delivery ADD CONSTRAINT uq_tracking UNIQUE (tracking_code);
ALTER TABLE delivery DROP CONSTRAINT chk_weight;
ALTER TABLE delivery RENAME TO shipment;

-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
-- DROP / TRUNCATE
-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS delivery CASCADE;  -- CASCADE drops dependent objects
TRUNCATE TABLE delivery RESTART IDENTITY CASCADE;  -- fast delete all rows

-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
-- INDEXES
-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
CREATE INDEX idx_delivery_status ON delivery(status);
CREATE INDEX idx_delivery_driver_date ON delivery(driver_id, scheduled_at DESC);
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx_tracking ON delivery(tracking_code);
CREATE INDEX idx_delivery_notes_gin ON delivery USING GIN (to_tsvector('english', notes));
CREATE INDEX idx_delivery_active ON delivery(scheduled_at) WHERE status = 'ACTIVE';  -- partial
CREATE INDEX idx_delivery_lower_code ON delivery(LOWER(tracking_code));  -- expression
DROP INDEX IF EXISTS idx_delivery_status;

-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
-- VIEWS
-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
CREATE VIEW active_deliveries AS
  SELECT id, tracking_code, driver_id, scheduled_at
  FROM delivery WHERE status = 'ACTIVE';

CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW daily_stats AS
  SELECT DATE(scheduled_at) AS day, COUNT(*) AS total, AVG(weight_kg) AS avg_weight
  FROM delivery GROUP BY DATE(scheduled_at);

REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW daily_stats;  -- must refresh manually
REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY daily_stats;  -- no lock (needs unique index)

-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
-- SEQUENCES
-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
CREATE SEQUENCE delivery_id_seq START 1 INCREMENT 1;
SELECT nextval('delivery_id_seq');
SELECT currval('delivery_id_seq');
ALTER SEQUENCE delivery_id_seq RESTART WITH 1000;

sections.heading: DML — Data Manipulation Language
sections.content:
-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
-- INSERT
-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
INSERT INTO delivery (tracking_code, status, driver_id, scheduled_at)
VALUES ('TRK-001', 'PLANNED', 42, '2026-07-21 08:00');

-- Insert multiple rows
INSERT INTO delivery (tracking_code, status, scheduled_at) VALUES
  ('TRK-002', 'PLANNED', '2026-07-21 09:00'),
  ('TRK-003', 'PLANNED', '2026-07-21 10:00'),
  ('TRK-004', 'PLANNED', '2026-07-21 11:00');

-- Insert from SELECT
INSERT INTO delivery_archive (id, tracking_code, status)
  SELECT id, tracking_code, status FROM delivery WHERE status = 'DELIVERED';

-- UPSERT (INSERT ... ON CONFLICT)
INSERT INTO delivery (tracking_code, status, scheduled_at)
VALUES ('TRK-001', 'DISPATCHED', NOW())
ON CONFLICT (tracking_code)
DO UPDATE SET status = EXCLUDED.status, updated_at = NOW();

INSERT INTO delivery (tracking_code, status)
VALUES ('TRK-001', 'PLANNED')
ON CONFLICT (tracking_code) DO NOTHING;  -- skip if exists

-- RETURNING (PostgreSQL) — get inserted/updated rows back
INSERT INTO delivery (tracking_code, status, scheduled_at)
VALUES ('TRK-005', 'PLANNED', NOW())
RETURNING id, tracking_code;

-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
-- UPDATE
-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
UPDATE delivery SET status = 'DISPATCHED', updated_at = NOW()
WHERE id = 123;

UPDATE delivery SET status = 'CANCELLED'
WHERE scheduled_at < NOW() - INTERVAL '7 days' AND status = 'PLANNED';

-- Update with subquery
UPDATE delivery d SET driver_id = (
  SELECT id FROM driver WHERE region = d.region ORDER BY workload LIMIT 1
) WHERE d.driver_id IS NULL;

-- Update with JOIN (PostgreSQL syntax)
UPDATE delivery d SET status = 'ASSIGNED'
FROM driver dr
WHERE d.driver_id = dr.id AND dr.available = true;

-- Update RETURNING
UPDATE delivery SET status = 'DISPATCHED'
WHERE id = 123 RETURNING id, status, driver_id;

-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
-- DELETE
-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
DELETE FROM delivery WHERE id = 123;
DELETE FROM delivery WHERE status = 'CANCELLED' AND created_at < '2025-01-01';

-- Delete with subquery
DELETE FROM delivery WHERE driver_id IN (
  SELECT id FROM driver WHERE terminated = true
);

-- Delete RETURNING
DELETE FROM delivery WHERE status = 'CANCELLED' RETURNING id, tracking_code;

-- Delete in batches (avoid long locks)
WITH batch AS (
  SELECT id FROM delivery WHERE status = 'ARCHIVED' LIMIT 1000
)
DELETE FROM delivery WHERE id IN (SELECT id FROM batch);

sections.heading: SELECT — Queries & Filtering
sections.content:
-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
-- BASIC SELECT
-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
SELECT * FROM delivery;                         -- all columns (avoid in production!)
SELECT id, status, scheduled_at FROM delivery;  -- explicit columns
SELECT DISTINCT status FROM delivery;           -- unique values
SELECT DISTINCT ON (driver_id) * FROM delivery  -- first row per driver (PostgreSQL)
  ORDER BY driver_id, scheduled_at DESC;

-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
-- WHERE — Filtering
-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
WHERE status = 'ACTIVE'                     -- equality
WHERE status != 'CANCELLED'                 -- not equal (also <>)
WHERE weight_kg > 10                        -- comparison: >, <, >=, <=
WHERE weight_kg BETWEEN 5 AND 20            -- range (inclusive)
WHERE status IN ('PLANNED', 'DISPATCHED')   -- set membership
WHERE status NOT IN ('CANCELLED', 'FAILED') -- exclusion
WHERE driver_id IS NULL                     -- null check
WHERE driver_id IS NOT NULL                 -- not null
WHERE tracking_code LIKE 'TRK-%'           -- pattern (% = any chars)
WHERE tracking_code LIKE 'TRK-00_'         -- pattern (_ = single char)
WHERE tracking_code ILIKE 'trk-%'          -- case-insensitive (PostgreSQL)
WHERE notes SIMILAR TO '%(urgent|express)%' -- regex-like (PostgreSQL)
WHERE scheduled_at >= CURRENT_DATE          -- date comparison
WHERE scheduled_at >= NOW() - INTERVAL '7 days'  -- relative date

-- Compound conditions
WHERE status = 'ACTIVE' AND driver_id IS NOT NULL
WHERE status = 'FAILED' OR (status = 'PLANNED' AND scheduled_at < NOW())
WHERE NOT (status = 'CANCELLED')

-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
-- ORDER BY
-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
ORDER BY scheduled_at ASC                   -- ascending (default)
ORDER BY scheduled_at DESC                  -- descending
ORDER BY status ASC, scheduled_at DESC      -- multi-column
ORDER BY CASE status                        -- custom order
  WHEN 'URGENT' THEN 1
  WHEN 'ACTIVE' THEN 2
  ELSE 3 END
ORDER BY scheduled_at NULLS LAST            -- nulls at end

-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
-- LIMIT & OFFSET (pagination)
-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
LIMIT 50                      -- first 50 rows
LIMIT 50 OFFSET 100           -- skip 100, return next 50
FETCH FIRST 10 ROWS ONLY     -- SQL standard syntax
FETCH NEXT 10 ROWS ONLY      -- same thing

sections.heading: JOINs
sections.content:
-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
-- JOIN TYPES
-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
-- INNER JOIN: only matching rows
SELECT d.id, dr.name
FROM delivery d
INNER JOIN driver dr ON d.driver_id = dr.id;

-- LEFT JOIN: all from left, NULL if no match on right
SELECT d.id, dr.name
FROM delivery d
LEFT JOIN driver dr ON d.driver_id = dr.id;

-- RIGHT JOIN: all from right (rarely used)
SELECT d.id, dr.name
FROM delivery d
RIGHT JOIN driver dr ON d.driver_id = dr.id;

-- FULL OUTER JOIN: all from both sides
SELECT d.id, dr.name
FROM delivery d
FULL OUTER JOIN driver dr ON d.driver_id = dr.id;

-- CROSS JOIN: cartesian product (every combo)
SELECT d.id, ts.slot
FROM delivery d
CROSS JOIN time_slot ts;

-- SELF JOIN: table joined to itself
SELECT d1.id, d2.id AS next_delivery
FROM delivery d1
JOIN delivery d2 ON d1.driver_id = d2.driver_id
  AND d2.scheduled_at > d1.scheduled_at;

-- LATERAL JOIN: subquery can reference outer table (PostgreSQL)
SELECT d.id, latest_event.event_type
FROM delivery d
LEFT JOIN LATERAL (
  SELECT event_type FROM delivery_event e
  WHERE e.delivery_id = d.id
  ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 1
) latest_event ON true;

-- Multiple JOINs
SELECT d.id, dr.name, r.region, COUNT(ds.id) AS stop_count
FROM delivery d
JOIN driver dr ON d.driver_id = dr.id
JOIN route r ON d.route_id = r.id
LEFT JOIN delivery_stop ds ON ds.delivery_id = d.id
GROUP BY d.id, dr.name, r.region;

-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
-- EXISTS / NOT EXISTS (correlated subquery)
-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
SELECT d.* FROM delivery d
WHERE EXISTS (
  SELECT 1 FROM delivery_stop ds
  WHERE ds.delivery_id = d.id AND ds.status = 'FAILED'
);

SELECT d.* FROM delivery d
WHERE NOT EXISTS (
  SELECT 1 FROM delivery_event e
  WHERE e.delivery_id = d.id AND e.event_type = 'CONFIRMED'
);

sections.heading: Aggregation — GROUP BY, HAVING, Aggregate Functions
sections.content:
-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
-- AGGREGATE FUNCTIONS
-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
SELECT
  COUNT(*)                    AS total_rows,
  COUNT(driver_id)            AS assigned_count,   -- excludes NULL
  COUNT(DISTINCT driver_id)   AS unique_drivers,
  SUM(weight_kg)              AS total_weight,
  AVG(weight_kg)              AS avg_weight,
  MIN(scheduled_at)           AS earliest,
  MAX(scheduled_at)           AS latest,
  BOOL_OR(is_express)         AS has_any_express,  -- PostgreSQL
  ARRAY_AGG(tracking_code)    AS all_codes,        -- PostgreSQL
  STRING_AGG(status, ', ')    AS statuses          -- PostgreSQL
FROM delivery;

-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
-- GROUP BY
-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
SELECT status, COUNT(*) AS total
FROM delivery
GROUP BY status;

SELECT driver_id, DATE(scheduled_at) AS day, COUNT(*) AS deliveries
FROM delivery
GROUP BY driver_id, DATE(scheduled_at)
ORDER BY day, deliveries DESC;

-- GROUP BY with expression
SELECT
  CASE WHEN weight_kg < 5 THEN 'light'
       WHEN weight_kg < 20 THEN 'medium'
       ELSE 'heavy' END AS category,
  COUNT(*)
FROM delivery
GROUP BY 1;  -- reference by position

-- GROUPING SETS / ROLLUP / CUBE (advanced aggregation)
SELECT status, driver_id, COUNT(*)
FROM delivery
GROUP BY GROUPING SETS (
  (status, driver_id),  -- detail
  (status),             -- subtotal by status
  ()                    -- grand total
);

SELECT status, driver_id, COUNT(*)
FROM delivery
GROUP BY ROLLUP (status, driver_id);  -- hierarchical subtotals

-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
-- HAVING — filter on aggregates
-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
SELECT driver_id, COUNT(*) AS total
FROM delivery
GROUP BY driver_id
HAVING COUNT(*) > 10;  -- only drivers with 10+ deliveries

SELECT status, AVG(weight_kg) AS avg_w
FROM delivery
GROUP BY status
HAVING AVG(weight_kg) > 15;

sections.heading: Window Functions
sections.content:
-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
-- WINDOW FUNCTIONS — compute across rows without collapsing
-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════

-- ROW_NUMBER: sequential number within partition
SELECT id, driver_id, scheduled_at,
  ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY driver_id ORDER BY scheduled_at) AS seq
FROM delivery;

-- RANK: same rank for ties, gaps after
SELECT id, weight_kg,
  RANK() OVER (ORDER BY weight_kg DESC) AS weight_rank
FROM delivery;

-- DENSE_RANK: same rank for ties, NO gaps
SELECT id, weight_kg,
  DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY weight_kg DESC) AS weight_rank
FROM delivery;

-- NTILE: divide into N equal buckets
SELECT id, weight_kg,
  NTILE(4) OVER (ORDER BY weight_kg) AS quartile
FROM delivery;

-- LAG / LEAD: access previous/next row
SELECT id, scheduled_at,
  LAG(scheduled_at) OVER (PARTITION BY driver_id ORDER BY scheduled_at) AS prev_delivery,
  LEAD(scheduled_at) OVER (PARTITION BY driver_id ORDER BY scheduled_at) AS next_delivery,
  scheduled_at - LAG(scheduled_at) OVER (PARTITION BY driver_id ORDER BY scheduled_at) AS gap
FROM delivery;

-- FIRST_VALUE / LAST_VALUE / NTH_VALUE
SELECT id, driver_id, scheduled_at,
  FIRST_VALUE(id) OVER (PARTITION BY driver_id ORDER BY scheduled_at) AS first_delivery,
  LAST_VALUE(id) OVER (
    PARTITION BY driver_id ORDER BY scheduled_at
    ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING
  ) AS last_delivery
FROM delivery;

-- Running totals / moving averages
SELECT id, weight_kg,
  SUM(weight_kg) OVER (ORDER BY id) AS running_total,
  AVG(weight_kg) OVER (ORDER BY id ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS moving_avg_3
FROM delivery;

-- Percent of total
SELECT driver_id, COUNT(*) AS cnt,
  ROUND(100.0 * COUNT(*) / SUM(COUNT(*)) OVER (), 1) AS pct
FROM delivery GROUP BY driver_id;

-- Named WINDOW (reuse definition)
SELECT id, driver_id, scheduled_at,
  ROW_NUMBER() OVER w AS seq,
  LAG(scheduled_at) OVER w AS prev
FROM delivery
WINDOW w AS (PARTITION BY driver_id ORDER BY scheduled_at);

sections.heading: Subqueries & CTEs
sections.content:
-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
-- SUBQUERIES
-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
-- Scalar subquery (returns single value)
SELECT id, weight_kg,
  weight_kg - (SELECT AVG(weight_kg) FROM delivery) AS diff_from_avg
FROM delivery;

-- IN subquery
SELECT * FROM delivery
WHERE driver_id IN (SELECT id FROM driver WHERE region = 'NORTH');

-- Correlated subquery (references outer query)
SELECT d.* FROM delivery d
WHERE d.weight_kg > (
  SELECT AVG(d2.weight_kg) FROM delivery d2
  WHERE d2.driver_id = d.driver_id  -- references outer 'd'
);

-- FROM subquery (derived table)
SELECT driver_id, avg_weight
FROM (
  SELECT driver_id, AVG(weight_kg) AS avg_weight
  FROM delivery GROUP BY driver_id
) sub
WHERE avg_weight > 15;

-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
-- CTEs (Common Table Expressions) — WITH clause
-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
WITH active_drivers AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT driver_id FROM delivery WHERE status = 'ACTIVE'
),
driver_stats AS (
  SELECT driver_id, COUNT(*) AS total, AVG(weight_kg) AS avg_w
  FROM delivery
  WHERE driver_id IN (SELECT driver_id FROM active_drivers)
  GROUP BY driver_id
)
SELECT ds.*, dr.name
FROM driver_stats ds
JOIN driver dr ON ds.driver_id = dr.id
ORDER BY ds.total DESC;

-- Recursive CTE (hierarchical data)
WITH RECURSIVE org_tree AS (
  -- Base case: top-level managers
  SELECT id, name, manager_id, 1 AS level
  FROM employee WHERE manager_id IS NULL
  UNION ALL
  -- Recursive step: employees under each manager
  SELECT e.id, e.name, e.manager_id, t.level + 1
  FROM employee e
  JOIN org_tree t ON e.manager_id = t.id
)
SELECT * FROM org_tree ORDER BY level, name;

-- CTE with INSERT (writable CTE — PostgreSQL)
WITH moved AS (
  DELETE FROM delivery WHERE status = 'ARCHIVED' RETURNING *
)
INSERT INTO delivery_archive SELECT * FROM moved;

sections.heading: Set Operations & CASE
sections.content:
-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
-- SET OPERATIONS
-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
-- UNION: combine results, remove duplicates
SELECT id, tracking_code FROM delivery WHERE status = 'URGENT'
UNION
SELECT id, tracking_code FROM delivery WHERE is_express = true;

-- UNION ALL: combine results, keep duplicates (faster!)
SELECT id, 'delivery' AS source FROM delivery
UNION ALL
SELECT id, 'return' AS source FROM delivery_return;

-- INTERSECT: rows in BOTH queries
SELECT driver_id FROM delivery WHERE status = 'ACTIVE'
INTERSECT
SELECT driver_id FROM delivery WHERE is_express = true;

-- EXCEPT: rows in first but NOT in second
SELECT driver_id FROM driver
EXCEPT
SELECT DISTINCT driver_id FROM delivery WHERE status = 'FAILED';

-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
-- CASE — conditional logic
-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
-- Simple CASE
SELECT id,
  CASE status
    WHEN 'PLANNED' THEN '📋 Planned'
    WHEN 'DISPATCHED' THEN '🚚 On the way'
    WHEN 'DELIVERED' THEN '✅ Done'
    ELSE '❓ Unknown'
  END AS status_label
FROM delivery;

-- Searched CASE (with conditions)
SELECT id, weight_kg,
  CASE
    WHEN weight_kg < 5 THEN 'light'
    WHEN weight_kg < 20 THEN 'medium'
    WHEN weight_kg < 50 THEN 'heavy'
    ELSE 'oversized'
  END AS weight_category
FROM delivery;

-- CASE in WHERE/ORDER BY
SELECT * FROM delivery
ORDER BY CASE WHEN status = 'URGENT' THEN 0 ELSE 1 END, scheduled_at;

-- COALESCE: first non-NULL value
SELECT COALESCE(driver_id, -1) AS driver_id FROM delivery;
SELECT COALESCE(notes, 'No notes') AS notes FROM delivery;

-- NULLIF: returns NULL if values are equal
SELECT NULLIF(status, 'UNKNOWN') FROM delivery;  -- treat 'UNKNOWN' as NULL

-- GREATEST / LEAST
SELECT GREATEST(scheduled_at, created_at) AS latest_date FROM delivery;

sections.heading: Date & Time Functions
sections.content:
-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
-- DATE/TIME — PostgreSQL
-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
-- Current timestamps
SELECT NOW();                           -- 2026-07-21 15:30:00+02
SELECT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP;               -- same as NOW()
SELECT CURRENT_DATE;                    -- 2026-07-21
SELECT CURRENT_TIME;                    -- 15:30:00+02
SELECT CLOCK_TIMESTAMP();               -- actual clock (changes within statement)

-- Extract parts
SELECT EXTRACT(YEAR FROM scheduled_at) FROM delivery;
SELECT EXTRACT(MONTH FROM scheduled_at) FROM delivery;
SELECT EXTRACT(DOW FROM scheduled_at) FROM delivery;   -- 0=Sun, 6=Sat
SELECT EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM scheduled_at) FROM delivery; -- unix timestamp
SELECT DATE_PART('hour', scheduled_at) FROM delivery;

-- Date arithmetic
SELECT scheduled_at + INTERVAL '2 hours' FROM delivery;
SELECT scheduled_at - INTERVAL '30 days' FROM delivery;
SELECT scheduled_at + 7 * INTERVAL '1 day' FROM delivery;
SELECT AGE(delivered_at, scheduled_at) FROM delivery;   -- interval between
SELECT NOW() - scheduled_at AS time_since FROM delivery;

-- Truncate to precision
SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', scheduled_at) FROM delivery; -- first of month
SELECT DATE_TRUNC('hour', scheduled_at) FROM delivery;  -- start of hour
SELECT DATE_TRUNC('week', CURRENT_DATE);                -- monday of week

-- Format
SELECT TO_CHAR(scheduled_at, 'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS') FROM delivery;
SELECT TO_CHAR(scheduled_at, 'Day, DD Mon YYYY') FROM delivery;

-- Parse
SELECT TO_TIMESTAMP('2026-07-21 15:30:00', 'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS');
SELECT TO_DATE('21/07/2026', 'DD/MM/YYYY');

-- Generate series (useful for reports with gaps)
SELECT generate_series(
  '2026-07-01'::date, '2026-07-31'::date, '1 day'::interval
) AS day;

-- Date ranges (PostgreSQL)
SELECT * FROM delivery
WHERE scheduled_at <@ tstzrange('2026-07-01', '2026-08-01');  -- contained in range

sections.heading: String Functions
sections.content:
-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
-- STRING FUNCTIONS
-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
SELECT LENGTH('hello');                    -- 5
SELECT UPPER('hello');                     -- HELLO
SELECT LOWER('HELLO');                     -- hello
SELECT INITCAP('hello world');             -- Hello World
SELECT TRIM('  hello  ');                  -- 'hello'
SELECT LTRIM('  hello');                   -- 'hello'
SELECT RTRIM('hello  ');                   -- 'hello'
SELECT REPLACE('hello world', 'world', 'SQL');  -- 'hello SQL'
SELECT SUBSTRING('hello' FROM 2 FOR 3);   -- 'ell'
SELECT LEFT('hello', 3);                   -- 'hel'
SELECT RIGHT('hello', 3);                  -- 'llo'
SELECT POSITION('lo' IN 'hello');          -- 4
SELECT SPLIT_PART('a.b.c', '.', 2);       -- 'b'
SELECT REPEAT('ha', 3);                   -- 'hahaha'
SELECT REVERSE('hello');                   -- 'olleh'
SELECT LPAD('42', 5, '0');                -- '00042'
SELECT RPAD('hi', 5, '.');                -- 'hi...'

-- Concatenation
SELECT 'Hello' || ' ' || 'World';         -- 'Hello World'
SELECT CONCAT('Hello', ' ', 'World');      -- 'Hello World' (handles NULL)
SELECT CONCAT_WS(', ', 'a', 'b', 'c');   -- 'a, b, c'

-- Pattern matching
SELECT 'hello' LIKE 'h%';                 -- true
SELECT 'hello' SIMILAR TO 'h(e|a)llo';   -- true (PostgreSQL regex-like)
SELECT 'hello' ~ '^h.*o$';               -- true (POSIX regex)
SELECT REGEXP_REPLACE('abc123', '[0-9]+', 'X');  -- 'abcX'
SELECT REGEXP_MATCHES('abc123def456', '[0-9]+', 'g');  -- {123}, {456}

-- Type casting
SELECT CAST('42' AS INTEGER);             -- 42
SELECT '42'::INTEGER;                     -- 42 (PostgreSQL shorthand)
SELECT 42::TEXT;                           -- '42'

sections.heading: Transactions & Locking
sections.content:
-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
-- TRANSACTIONS
-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
BEGIN;  -- or START TRANSACTION
  UPDATE delivery SET status = 'DISPATCHED' WHERE id = 123;
  INSERT INTO delivery_event (delivery_id, event_type) VALUES (123, 'DISPATCHED');
COMMIT;

BEGIN;
  UPDATE account SET balance = balance - 100 WHERE id = 1;
  UPDATE account SET balance = balance + 100 WHERE id = 2;
  -- something goes wrong:
ROLLBACK;  -- undo everything

-- SAVEPOINT (partial rollback)
BEGIN;
  INSERT INTO delivery (tracking_code) VALUES ('TRK-A');
  SAVEPOINT sp1;
  INSERT INTO delivery (tracking_code) VALUES ('TRK-B');
  ROLLBACK TO sp1;  -- undo TRK-B only
  INSERT INTO delivery (tracking_code) VALUES ('TRK-C');
COMMIT;  -- TRK-A and TRK-C are committed

-- Isolation levels
SET TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL READ COMMITTED;    -- PostgreSQL default
SET TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL REPEATABLE READ;
SET TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL SERIALIZABLE;

BEGIN ISOLATION LEVEL SERIALIZABLE;
  -- all reads see a consistent snapshot
  -- conflicts are detected at commit
COMMIT;

-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
-- LOCKING
-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
-- SELECT FOR UPDATE: lock rows for modification
SELECT * FROM delivery WHERE id = 123 FOR UPDATE;

-- SKIP LOCKED: skip rows locked by other transactions (queue pattern)
SELECT * FROM delivery
WHERE status = 'PENDING'
ORDER BY created_at
LIMIT 1
FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED;

-- NOWAIT: fail immediately if row is locked
SELECT * FROM delivery WHERE id = 123 FOR UPDATE NOWAIT;

-- Advisory locks (application-level)
SELECT pg_advisory_lock(12345);      -- blocking
SELECT pg_try_advisory_lock(12345);  -- non-blocking, returns bool
SELECT pg_advisory_unlock(12345);

sections.heading: PostgreSQL-Specific Features
sections.content:
-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
-- JSONB (PostgreSQL)
-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
-- Store and query JSON
CREATE TABLE delivery_meta (
  delivery_id BIGINT PRIMARY KEY,
  metadata    JSONB NOT NULL DEFAULT '{}'
);

INSERT INTO delivery_meta VALUES (1, '{"priority": "high", "tags": ["express", "fragile"]}');

-- Access fields
SELECT metadata->>'priority' FROM delivery_meta;           -- text: 'high'
SELECT metadata->'tags' FROM delivery_meta;                -- jsonb: ["express","fragile"]
SELECT metadata->'tags'->>0 FROM delivery_meta;            -- text: 'express'
SELECT metadata #>> '{tags,0}' FROM delivery_meta;         -- text: 'express' (path)

-- Filter
SELECT * FROM delivery_meta WHERE metadata->>'priority' = 'high';
SELECT * FROM delivery_meta WHERE metadata @> '{"priority": "high"}';
SELECT * FROM delivery_meta WHERE metadata ? 'priority';   -- key exists
SELECT * FROM delivery_meta WHERE metadata->'tags' ? 'express'; -- array contains

-- Update JSONB
UPDATE delivery_meta SET metadata = metadata || '{"urgent": true}';    -- merge
UPDATE delivery_meta SET metadata = metadata - 'priority';             -- remove key
UPDATE delivery_meta SET metadata = jsonb_set(metadata, '{priority}', '"low"');

-- Index JSONB
CREATE INDEX idx_meta_gin ON delivery_meta USING GIN (metadata);
CREATE INDEX idx_meta_priority ON delivery_meta ((metadata->>'priority'));

-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
-- ARRAYS (PostgreSQL)
-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
SELECT ARRAY[1, 2, 3];                    -- {1,2,3}
SELECT ARRAY_AGG(id) FROM delivery;       -- aggregate into array
SELECT UNNEST(ARRAY[1,2,3]);              -- expand array to rows
SELECT * FROM delivery WHERE 42 = ANY(stop_ids);  -- array contains
SELECT * FROM delivery WHERE stop_ids @> ARRAY[42]; -- contains
SELECT ARRAY_LENGTH(stop_ids, 1) FROM delivery;  -- array length

-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
-- PARTITIONING (PostgreSQL 10+)
-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
CREATE TABLE events (
  id BIGSERIAL, event_time TIMESTAMPTZ, payload JSONB
) PARTITION BY RANGE (event_time);

CREATE TABLE events_2026_q3 PARTITION OF events
  FOR VALUES FROM ('2026-07-01') TO ('2026-10-01');

-- List partition
CREATE TABLE delivery_by_region (
  id BIGSERIAL, region TEXT, data JSONB
) PARTITION BY LIST (region);

CREATE TABLE delivery_north PARTITION OF delivery_by_region
  FOR VALUES IN ('NORTH', 'NORTHEAST');

-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
-- OTHER USEFUL PostgreSQL FEATURES
-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════
-- EXPLAIN ANALYZE: show execution plan with actual timing
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS, FORMAT TEXT)
  SELECT * FROM delivery WHERE status = 'ACTIVE';

-- Copy (bulk import/export)
COPY delivery FROM '/path/to/file.csv' WITH (FORMAT csv, HEADER true);
COPY (SELECT * FROM delivery WHERE status = 'ACTIVE') TO '/tmp/active.csv' CSV HEADER;

-- Generate series
SELECT * FROM generate_series(1, 10) AS n;  -- numbers 1..10
SELECT * FROM generate_series('2026-01-01'::date, '2026-12-31'::date, '1 month');

-- Conditional aggregation (pivot-like)
SELECT driver_id,
  COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE status = 'DELIVERED') AS delivered,
  COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE status = 'FAILED') AS failed,
  COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE status = 'ACTIVE') AS active
FROM delivery GROUP BY driver_id;

sections.heading: JOIN types visualized
sections.content:
-- INNER JOIN: only matching rows from BOTH tables
SELECT d.id, r.name
FROM delivery d
INNER JOIN route r ON d.route_id = r.id;
-- Only deliveries that HAVE a route

-- LEFT JOIN: all from left + matches from right (NULL if no match)
SELECT d.id, dr.name
FROM delivery d
LEFT JOIN driver dr ON d.driver_id = dr.id;
-- ALL deliveries, even those without a driver (driver columns = NULL)

-- RIGHT JOIN: all from right + matches from left (rarely used)
-- FULL OUTER JOIN: all from both sides (NULLs where no match)
When to use each: • INNER JOIN: "I only want rows that exist in BOTH tables" • LEFT JOIN: "I want ALL deliveries, even those without a driver" • EXISTS: "I want deliveries that have at least one stop" (better than JOIN when you don't need the joined data) 
-- EXISTS (often faster than JOIN for checking existence)
SELECT d.*
FROM delivery d
WHERE EXISTS (
    SELECT 1 FROM delivery_stop ds
    WHERE ds.delivery_id = d.id AND ds.status = 'FAILED'
);
-- "Find deliveries that have at least one failed stop"

sections.heading: Common JOIN pitfalls
sections.content:
1. Cartesian product (missing ON clause): 
-- ❌ 1000 deliveries × 500 drivers = 500,000 rows!
SELECT * FROM delivery, driver;

-- ✓ Always specify the join condition
SELECT * FROM delivery d JOIN driver dr ON d.driver_id = dr.id;
2. Duplicate rows from 1:N joins: 
-- If delivery has 5 stops, you get 5 rows per delivery
SELECT d.id, d.status, ds.stop_name
FROM delivery d
JOIN delivery_stop ds ON ds.delivery_id = d.id;

-- Solution: aggregate or use DISTINCT/GROUP BY depending on need
SELECT d.id, d.status, COUNT(ds.id) as stop_count
FROM delivery d
JOIN delivery_stop ds ON ds.delivery_id = d.id
GROUP BY d.id, d.status;
3. NULL in JOIN conditions: NULL never equals anything (not even NULL). LEFT JOIN handles this correctly (returns NULL columns). INNER JOIN silently drops rows where the join column is NULL.

title: Indexes — How Databases Find Data Fast

sections.heading: What is an index?
sections.content:
An index is a sorted data structure (B-tree) that allows the database to find rows without scanning the entire table. 
-- Without index: Sequential Scan (reads ALL rows)
-- 1 million deliveries → reads 1 million rows to find one
SELECT * FROM delivery WHERE tracking_code = 'TRK-4567';

-- With index: Index Scan (reads ~3-4 pages in B-tree)
CREATE INDEX idx_delivery_tracking ON delivery(tracking_code);
-- Now the same query reads ~4 rows instead of 1,000,000
Types of indexes: • B-tree (default): equality and range queries (=, <, >, BETWEEN) • Hash: equality only (=), slightly faster for exact matches • GIN: arrays, JSONB, full-text search • GiST: geometric, range types Composite index: 
-- Leftmost prefix rule: index on (A, B, C) can be used for:
-- WHERE A = ?          ✓
-- WHERE A = ? AND B = ? ✓
-- WHERE A = ? AND B = ? AND C = ? ✓
-- WHERE B = ?          ✗ (skipped A)
-- WHERE C = ?          ✗ (skipped A and B)
CREATE INDEX idx_delivery_status_date ON delivery(status, scheduled_at);

sections.heading: When indexes hurt
sections.content:
Indexes are NOT free: • Each INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE must update the index • Indexes consume disk space • Too many indexes slow down writes Don't index: • Columns with very few distinct values (boolean, status with 3 values on small tables) • Tables with <1000 rows (seq scan is faster) • Columns rarely used in WHERE/JOIN/ORDER BY Always index: • Primary keys (automatic) • Foreign keys (JOIN performance) • Columns in WHERE clauses of frequent queries • Columns in ORDER BY of paginated queries EXPLAIN ANALYZE — see the execution plan: 
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT * FROM delivery WHERE status = 'PLANNED' AND route_id = 42;

-- Look for:
-- "Seq Scan" → no index used (bad for large tables)
-- "Index Scan" → good, using an index
-- "Bitmap Heap Scan" → good for multiple conditions
-- "Nested Loop" → check if inner side has index
-- "Sort" → expensive without index on ORDER BY column

sections.heading: ACID properties
sections.content:
Atomicity — All or nothing. If any statement fails, ALL are rolled back. Consistency — DB moves from one valid state to another. Isolation — Concurrent transactions don't interfere with each other. Durability — Once committed, data survives crashes. 
BEGIN;
  UPDATE delivery SET status = 'DISPATCHED' WHERE id = 4567;
  INSERT INTO delivery_event (delivery_id, event_type) VALUES (4567, 'DISPATCHED');
  UPDATE driver SET active_delivery_id = 4567 WHERE id = 42;
COMMIT;
-- Either ALL three succeed, or NONE are applied
In Spring Boot: 
@Transactional
public void dispatch(Long deliveryId, Long driverId) {
    deliveryRepo.updateStatus(deliveryId, DISPATCHED);
    eventRepo.save(new DeliveryEvent(deliveryId, DISPATCHED));
    driverRepo.assignDelivery(driverId, deliveryId);
    // If any throws → ALL rolled back automatically
}

sections.heading: Isolation levels
sections.content:
READ UNCOMMITTED → Can see uncommitted data from other TX (dirty reads)
READ COMMITTED   → Only sees committed data (PostgreSQL default)
REPEATABLE READ  → Same query returns same results within TX
SERIALIZABLE     → Full isolation (as if sequential execution)
Common problems: • Dirty read: TX1 updates, TX2 reads it, TX1 rolls back → TX2 has phantom data • Non-repeatable read: TX1 reads, TX2 commits update, TX1 re-reads → different value • Phantom read: TX1 counts rows, TX2 inserts, TX1 counts again → different count PostgreSQL default: READ COMMITTED — safe from dirty reads but allows non-repeatable reads. For most web applications this is correct. When to use higher isolation: • Financial calculations that must be consistent → SERIALIZABLE • Reports that need snapshot consistency → REPEATABLE READ • Normal CRUD operations → READ COMMITTED (default, best performance)

title: SQL Performance — The N+1 Problem & Optimization

sections.heading: The N+1 query problem
Query optimization checklist
sections.content:
Optimize by evidence, not by folklore. The useful order is: measure the slow request, inspect its plan, make one change, then measure again with representative data. This is more reliable than adding an index or rewriting a query because it "looks faster". 1. Return and process less data: 
-- ❌ Reads wide rows and transfers columns the screen never uses
SELECT * FROM delivery WHERE status = 'PLANNED';

-- ✓ Small projection, stable order and a bounded result
SELECT id, status, driver_id, scheduled_at
FROM delivery
WHERE status = 'PLANNED'
ORDER BY scheduled_at, id
LIMIT 50;
Use a DTO projection in JPA when the screen is read-only. `LIMIT` is not a substitute for pagination, but every user-facing list needs a deliberate bound. 2. Keep predicates sargable: an indexed column must be searchable without applying a function or implicit cast to every row. 
-- ❌ Function wraps the indexed column
WHERE EXTRACT(YEAR FROM scheduled_at) = 2026

-- ✓ Range predicate can use an index on scheduled_at
WHERE scheduled_at >= TIMESTAMPTZ '2026-01-01'
  AND scheduled_at <  TIMESTAMPTZ '2027-01-01'
The same principle applies to `LOWER(email)`, leading-wildcard `LIKE '%term'`, mismatched data types and arithmetic on a column. If the expression is genuinely required, consider an expression index — after measuring. 3. Design an index for the access path: start from the frequent `WHERE`, `JOIN`, `ORDER BY` and selected columns together. For a list filtered by status and ordered by date, `(status, scheduled_at, id)` is a plausible candidate; its leftmost columns matter. Indexing every column or every low-cardinality value simply makes writes and maintenance more expensive. 4. Treat EXISTS, IN, JOIN and CTEs as semantic choices first: `EXISTS` expresses "does a related row exist?" and avoids duplicate parent rows. `JOIN` is right when columns from both relations are needed. PostgreSQL can optimize `IN` and a non-materialized CTE well, so do not assume one is always faster — inspect the plan. A CTE is often a readability tool; `MATERIALIZED` deliberately changes that trade-off.

sections.heading: Read the plan before changing SQL
sections.content:
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS)
SELECT d.id, d.status, d.scheduled_at
FROM delivery d
WHERE d.status = 'PLANNED'
  AND d.scheduled_at >= now() - interval '7 days'
ORDER BY d.scheduled_at, d.id
LIMIT 50;
Read a PostgreSQL plan in this order: • Actual time and loops: find the node doing the real work, not merely the node at the top. A cheap operation repeated thousands of times is expensive. • Estimated rows vs actual rows: a large difference usually points to stale statistics, correlated columns or a poor predicate estimate. Run `ANALYZE` where appropriate; only then consider extended statistics or a query/index change. • Access path: a sequential scan is not automatically bad — it is often correct for a small table or a query returning most rows. It becomes suspicious when it reads a large table to return a tiny fraction. • Sort, hash and temporary I/O: external disk sorts, large hash batches and repeated nested-loop inner scans are concrete leads. Check filters, join indexes and row counts before forcing a plan. • Buffers: shared hits versus reads tell you whether the issue is CPU/planning or storage I/O. `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` executes the statement. Use it carefully with writes and production data; prefer a transaction that you can roll back for investigative DML.

sections.heading: Joins, pagination and repeated work
sections.content:
Joins: index join keys on the large/inner side, filter early when it reduces rows, and project only what is needed. Do not manually reorder joins to outsmart PostgreSQL without evidence; the planner can reorder many joins, while stale statistics can make it choose poorly. Pagination: offset pagination is simple and supports jumping to a page, but later offsets still require the database to find and discard earlier rows. Cursor/keyset pagination is usually the better default for a "next page" feed. 
-- Keyset pagination: stable, bounded work per next page
SELECT id, status, scheduled_at
FROM delivery
WHERE (scheduled_at, id) > (:lastScheduledAt, :lastId)
ORDER BY scheduled_at, id
LIMIT 50;
The ordering must be deterministic and supported by an index such as `(scheduled_at, id)`. Sorting and aggregation: `ORDER BY`, `GROUP BY`, `DISTINCT` and `UNION` can require substantial memory, disk or deduplication work. Remove them when the product does not need them; use `UNION ALL` only when duplicates are valid. For expensive, repeated reporting aggregates, evaluate a materialized view or a pre-aggregation pipeline and define its refresh/freshness contract. Production guardrails: set a sensible statement timeout, observe slow-query metrics, keep statistics current, and verify the improvement at production-like cardinality. An index that speeds up a read can increase write latency, vacuum work and storage — that trade-off belongs in the decision.

sections.heading: Senior Perspective
sections.content:
Senior engineers don't just write SQL — they read execution plans, understand the query planner's decisions, and design schemas for their access patterns.

Key distinctions:
• An index that improves one query may degrade writes across the table
• EXPLAIN ANALYZE is the single most important debugging tool
• The difference between a 100ms query and a 10s query is often one missing composite index
• Isolation levels are a trade-off between correctness and throughput, not a default to set and forget
• Connection pooling, statement timeouts and lock monitoring are production essentials

sections.heading: REWE Perspective
sections.content:
In TRAB's delivery database:

• Route planning queries join deliveries × stops × drivers × time windows
• Indexes must support both real-time lookups (by driver, by route) and batch reporting (by date range, by warehouse)
• PostgreSQL's MVCC means long-running reports don't block transactional inserts — but they can bloat the table
• Partitioning delivery_events by month keeps recent data fast while archiving history
• The N+1 problem appears when loading route details for a list of deliveries — solved with JOIN FETCH or batch loading

sections.heading: When NOT to use
sections.content:
When NOT to optimize SQL prematurely:

• Don't add indexes before measuring — each index costs write performance and storage
• Don't denormalize before proving the JOIN is actually the bottleneck
• Don't use database-level caching when application-level caching (Redis) is more appropriate
• Don't use SERIALIZABLE isolation unless you can prove the anomaly you're preventing
• Don't use stored procedures for business logic that changes frequently

title: Storage Engines — B-Trees vs LSM-Trees (DDIA Ch.3)

sections.heading: Why storage engines matter for application developers
sections.content: You won't implement a storage engine, but you must choose one appropriate for your workload. The two dominant families: • B-Trees (PostgreSQL, MySQL InnoDB, Oracle) — page-oriented, in-place updates • LSM-Trees (Cassandra, RocksDB, LevelDB, HBase) — log-structured, append-only, compaction The choice affects read/write performance, space amplification, and compaction overhead. Understanding the internals lets you predict behavior under load and tune accordingly. Key insight from Kleppmann: "Well-chosen indexes speed up read queries, but every index slows down writes. Databases don't index everything by default — you choose indexes using your knowledge of the application's query patterns."

sections.heading: B-Trees — the workhorse of relational databases
sections.content:
How B-Trees work: • Data organized in fixed-size pages (typically 4KB) • Each page can be identified by an address (like a pointer on disk) • Pages form a tree: root → internal nodes → leaf nodes containing actual values • Lookups traverse from root to leaf: O(log n) — typically 3-4 levels for millions of rows • Branching factor ~500: a 4-level tree can store up to 256 TB 
-- B-tree lookup for: WHERE id = 42
Root page → "id between 1-100 is in page 3"
  Page 3 → "id between 40-60 is in page 17"
    Page 17 → found row with id=42 (leaf page)
-- Only 3 disk reads for millions of rows!
Write operations: • Find the leaf page containing the key • Overwrite the page in place with the new value • If page is full → split into two half-full pages, update parent • Uses Write-Ahead Log (WAL) for crash recovery — every modification written to append-only log BEFORE updating tree pages Strengths: • Excellent read performance (O(log n)) • Predictable latency • Efficient range queries (leaf pages linked) • Mature, well-understood, decades of optimization Weaknesses: • Write amplification: one logical write = WAL write + page write (possibly + page split) • Fragmentation over time • Concurrent access requires careful locking (latches on pages)

sections.heading: LSM-Trees — optimized for write-heavy workloads
sections.content:
The core idea: never modify data in place. All writes go to an in-memory balanced tree (memtable). When memtable is full, flush to disk as a sorted file (SSTable). SSTable (Sorted String Table): • Key-value pairs sorted by key • Immutable once written • Merging is efficient (merge-sort of sorted files) • Sparse index in memory (one entry per few KB) 
Write path:
1. Write to in-memory memtable (Red-Black tree / AVL)
2. When memtable reaches threshold (~MB) → flush to disk as SSTable
3. Background compaction: merge SSTables, discard old versions

Read path:
1. Check memtable
2. Check most recent SSTable on disk
3. Check next SSTable... (use Bloom filters to skip)
Compaction strategies: • Size-tiered (Cassandra default): merge SSTables of similar size → good write throughput, higher space amplification • Leveled (RocksDB, LevelDB): organized in levels of increasing size → better read performance, more compaction I/O Strengths: • Sequential writes only → much faster on HDD and SSD (10x-100x write throughput vs B-tree) • Better compression (sorted data compresses well) • No fragmentation (compaction produces contiguous files) Weaknesses: • Reads slower (must check multiple SSTables) • Compaction can interfere with ongoing reads/writes (I/O bandwidth) • Unpredictable tail latency during compaction • Space amplification: old versions exist until compacted away

sections.heading: Decision matrix: when to choose which
sections.content: Choose B-Tree (PostgreSQL, MySQL) when: • Read-heavy workload (OLTP with many lookups) • Need predictable latency (p99 matters) • Range queries are common • Transactions with strong isolation required • Data fits in memory or working set is hot Choose LSM-Tree (Cassandra, RocksDB) when: • Write-heavy workload (event logging, time-series, IoT) • Write throughput is the bottleneck • Can tolerate slightly higher read latency • Data is append-mostly (logs, events, metrics) • Need better space efficiency For a REWE delivery system: • Main OLTP database (orders, drivers, routes): B-Tree (PostgreSQL) — reads dominate, transactions critical • Event store (GPS pings, delivery events, telemetry): LSM-Tree — write-heavy, append-only, eventual consistency OK • Search index (Elasticsearch uses LSM segments internally) Interview insight: "The choice between B-Tree and LSM isn't about which is 'better' — it's about matching the storage engine to your access pattern. B-Trees optimize for reads; LSM-Trees optimize for writes. Most production systems use both for different data."

sections.heading: OLTP vs OLAP — transaction processing vs analytics
sections.content:
DDIA makes a crucial distinction between two storage patterns: 
                OLTP (row-oriented)          OLAP (column-oriented)
Read pattern    Small number of records       Aggregate over many rows
                by key (point lookups)        (scan large datasets)
Write pattern   Random-access, low-latency    Bulk import (ETL) or event
                user-driven inserts           streaming
Used by         End users via web app         Internal analysts, BI tools
Data size       GB to low TB                  TB to PB
Bottleneck      Disk seek time                Disk bandwidth
Example         PostgreSQL, MySQL              BigQuery, Redshift, Clickhouse
Column-oriented storage (OLAP): • Stores all values of a column together (not all columns of a row) • Enables incredible compression (run-length encoding, bitmap encoding) • Reads only the columns needed for the query • Vectorized processing: CPU operates on compressed column vectors 
-- This query in column store reads ONLY status and scheduled_at columns:
SELECT status, COUNT(*) FROM delivery
WHERE scheduled_at >= '2026-01-01'
GROUP BY status;
-- In row store: reads ENTIRE rows (40 columns) just to use 2
Materialized views and data cubes: Pre-computed aggregates for common queries. Trade storage and write cost for instant reads. DDIA calls this "aggregation: data cubes and materialized views."

title: Replication — Keeping Data Consistent Across Machines

sections.heading: Why replicate?
sections.content: Three reasons to keep copies of data on multiple machines: 1. High availability — if one node dies, others continue serving 2. Latency — serve users from geographically close replicas 3. Read scalability — distribute read load across replicas All difficulty in replication lies in handling changes. If data never changed, you'd copy it once and be done. The challenge is keeping replicas consistent as writes happen. Three main approaches: • Leader-based (single-leader) • Multi-leader • Leaderless

sections.heading: Leader-Follower replication (single-leader)
sections.content:
The most common approach (PostgreSQL, MySQL, Oracle, MongoDB): 
┌─────────┐    writes    ┌─────────┐
│  Client │────────────→│  Leader │
└─────────┘             └────┬────┘
                             │ replication stream
                    ┌────────┼────────┐
                    ▼        ▼        ▼
              ┌──────┐ ┌──────┐ ┌──────┐
              │Foll.1│ │Foll.2│ │Foll.3│
              └──────┘ └──────┘ └──────┘
                  ▲        ▲        ▲
              ┌───┴────────┴────────┴───┐
              │    Clients read from     │
              │    leader OR followers   │
              └─────────────────────────┘
Rules: • Only leader accepts writes • Followers receive replication log and apply changes in same order • Reads can go to any replica (leader or follower) Synchronous vs asynchronous replication: • Synchronous: leader waits for follower to confirm → guaranteed up-to-date, but any follower outage blocks writes • Asynchronous: leader doesn't wait → fast writes, but follower may lag (data loss if leader crashes) • Semi-synchronous (practical): one follower sync + others async PostgreSQL streaming replication: 
-- Primary sends WAL records to standby in real-time
-- Standby applies WAL and can serve read queries
-- Typical lag: milliseconds to seconds
-- pg_stat_replication shows lag per replica

sections.heading: Replication lag — the consistency problems
sections.content:
When using async replication, followers can be seconds behind the leader. This creates observable anomalies: 1. Reading your own writes (read-after-write consistency): 
-- User updates their profile (goes to leader)
UPDATE user_profile SET name = 'Alice' WHERE id = 42;

-- User immediately refreshes page (reads from follower)
SELECT name FROM user_profile WHERE id = 42;
-- May still return old name if follower hasn't caught up!
Solutions: • Read user's own data from leader • Track write timestamp, route reads to replica that's caught up • Client remembers its last write timestamp 2. Monotonic reads: User sees data, refreshes, sees OLDER data (different follower, further behind). Solution: pin user to one replica (session affinity). 3. Consistent prefix reads: Causal order violated — seeing an answer before the question. Solution: write causally related data to same partition. Interview answer framework: "Replication lag is an unavoidable consequence of asynchronous replication. The application must be designed to tolerate it — either by routing specific reads to the leader, or by building the UI to handle eventual consistency gracefully."

sections.heading: Multi-Leader replication
sections.content:
When: Multiple datacenters, each with its own leader. Writes accepted at any datacenter. 
DC Europe          DC US-East         DC Asia
┌─────────┐       ┌─────────┐       ┌─────────┐
│ Leader  │◄─────►│ Leader  │◄─────►│ Leader  │
│+Followers│      │+Followers│      │+Followers│
└─────────┘       └─────────┘       └─────────┘
   writes↑           writes↑           writes↑
Advantages: • Tolerates entire datacenter outage • Lower write latency (local leader) • Network interruptions between DCs don't block local writes The BIG problem: write conflicts 
-- User edits title simultaneously in two DCs
DC-Europe: UPDATE doc SET title = 'Title A' WHERE id = 1;
DC-US:     UPDATE doc SET title = 'Title B' WHERE id = 1;
-- Both succeed locally. Which one wins when they replicate?
Conflict resolution strategies: • Last Write Wins (LWW): use timestamp → simple but loses data (Cassandra default) • Merge values: keep both and let application resolve (CRDTs) • Custom logic: application-specific handlers • Conflict avoidance: route all writes for a record to same leader Practical uses: collaborative editing (Google Docs), mobile offline-first apps, cross-region deployment.

sections.heading: Leaderless replication (Dynamo-style)
sections.content:
No leader at all. Client writes to multiple replicas directly (Cassandra, Riak, DynamoDB). 
Write: send to ALL replicas, succeed if W out of N acknowledge
Read:  read from ALL replicas, succeed if R out of N respond

Quorum condition: W + R > N guarantees overlap
-- With N=3, W=2, R=2: always read at least one up-to-date copy
Example with N=3, W=2, R=2: 
Client writes value v2 to all 3 replicas:
  Replica A: ✓ (has v2)
  Replica B: ✓ (has v2)  → W=2 met, write succeeds
  Replica C: ✗ (still has v1, was temporarily down)

Client reads from all 3:
  Replica A: returns v2
  Replica B: returns v2  → R=2 met, client sees v2 ✓
  Replica C: returns v1 (stale)
Repair mechanisms: • Read repair: client detects stale value during read, writes back to stale replica • Anti-entropy: background process comparing replicas and copying missing data Limitations of quorums: Even with W + R > N, edge cases can return stale reads: • Sloppy quorums (writes go to nodes outside the designated N) • Concurrent writes (which one wins?) • Write concurrent with read (may see old or new) • Partial failures during writes When to use: High availability requirements, tolerate eventual consistency, multi-datacenter without a single leader. Not ideal for strong consistency needs (banking, inventory).

sections.heading: Senior perspective — choosing replication strategy
sections.content: Decision framework for interview: | Requirement | Strategy | |---|---| | Strong consistency, single region | Single leader (PostgreSQL streaming) | | High availability, tolerate lag | Single leader + async followers | | Multi-region writes | Multi-leader (accept conflict complexity) | | Maximum availability, eventual consistency OK | Leaderless (Cassandra/DynamoDB) | | Read scalability | Add read replicas to any strategy | Key Kleppmann quotes for interviews: • "If the leader fails and is not recoverable, any writes that have not yet been replicated to followers are lost." • "Pretending that replication is synchronous when in fact it is asynchronous is a recipe for problems down the line." • "There is no general solution to conflict resolution... the application developer must resolve conflicts in a way that makes sense for the business logic." REWE application: • Delivery tracking database: single-leader PostgreSQL with streaming replication (strong consistency for order state) • Analytics/reporting: read replicas in each region • Driver GPS events: could use leaderless for maximum availability (stale position is better than no position)

title: Partitioning & Sharding — Scaling Beyond One Machine

sections.heading: Why partition data?
sections.content:
When data grows too large for one machine, split it into partitions (also called shards, regions, tablets, vnodes). Each partition is a small database of its own — lives on one node (with replication for fault tolerance). 
Before: 1 node holds 10 TB
After:  10 nodes hold 1 TB each (partitioned)

         ┌──────────────────────────────┐
         │   Query Router / Client      │
         └─────┬──────┬──────┬──────────┘
               │      │      │
          ┌────▼─┐ ┌──▼──┐ ┌─▼────┐
          │Part A│ │Part B│ │Part C│
          │A-H   │ │I-P   │ │Q-Z   │
          └──────┘ └─────┘ └──────┘
Goal: spread data and query load evenly. Avoid hot spots (one partition getting disproportionate traffic). Partitioning is usually combined with replication: each partition has multiple replicas on different nodes.

sections.heading: Partitioning by Key Range
sections.content:
Assign a continuous range of keys to each partition (like volumes of an encyclopedia). 
Partition 1: delivery_id 1 - 100,000
Partition 2: delivery_id 100,001 - 200,000
Partition 3: delivery_id 200,001 - 300,000

Or by date:
Partition Jan-2026: events where timestamp in January
Partition Feb-2026: events where timestamp in February
Advantages: • Efficient range scans (all data for a range is on one partition) • Easy to understand boundaries Disadvantages: • Hot spots: if writes have a natural ordering (timestamps), all writes go to the LATEST partition while others sit idle • Need manual or automatic rebalancing as data grows Mitigation for time-series hot spots: 
-- Instead of partitioning by timestamp alone:
-- Prefix with sensor_id or region → spreads writes
Partition key = sensor_name + date
-- sensor_A_2026-07-21, sensor_B_2026-07-21 → different partitions
Used by: HBase, BigTable, RethinkDB, MongoDB (with ranged shard key)

sections.heading: Partitioning by Hash of Key
sections.content:
Apply a hash function to the key, assign hash ranges to partitions. Eliminates hot spots from ordered keys. 
hash(key) → 0 to 2^32
Partition A: hash 0 - 1,073,741,823
Partition B: hash 1,073,741,824 - 2,147,483,647
Partition C: hash 2,147,483,648 - 3,221,225,471
Partition D: hash 3,221,225,472 - 4,294,967,295
Advantages: • Even distribution regardless of key patterns • No hot spots (even sequential keys get spread out) Disadvantages: • Loses range query efficiency: adjacent keys end up on different partitions • Range scans require querying ALL partitions (scatter/gather) Compromise: compound partition key (Cassandra) 
-- First part hashed (for distribution), rest sorted within partition
PRIMARY KEY ((driver_id), delivery_date, delivery_id)
-- All deliveries for a driver on same partition (hashed by driver_id)
-- Within that partition, sorted by date → efficient range scan per driver
Used by: Cassandra, DynamoDB, Riak, Voldemort

sections.heading: Secondary indexes with partitioning
sections.content:
Partitioning gets complicated when you need secondary indexes (not just lookup by primary key). Approach 1: Local index (document-partitioned) Each partition maintains its own secondary index covering only its data. 
Partition 0:                 Partition 1:
deliveries id 0-499         deliveries id 500-999
local index: status         local index: status
  PLANNED → [1,7,42]        PLANNED → [501,622]
  DELIVERED → [3,9]          DELIVERED → [503,700]
• Write: update only local index (fast) • Read by secondary index: scatter/gather — query ALL partitions, merge results • Used by: MongoDB, Riak, Cassandra, Elasticsearch, Solr Approach 2: Global index (term-partitioned) The index itself is partitioned — by the indexed term, not by document. 
Index partition A (status A-M):    Index partition B (status N-Z):
  DELIVERED → [3,503,700]           PLANNED → [1,7,42,501,622]
• Read: go to one index partition (fast reads) • Write: may need to update multiple index partitions (slower, often async) • Used by: DynamoDB global secondary indexes, Riak search Interview insight: "There's always a trade-off. Local indexes make writes fast but reads expensive. Global indexes make reads fast but writes require distributed coordination."

sections.heading: Rebalancing — what happens when partitions move
sections.content: Triggers for rebalancing: • Add a new node (scale out) • Remove a failed node • Data grows unevenly Strategies: 1. Fixed number of partitions: • Create many more partitions than nodes (e.g., 1000 partitions for 10 nodes → 100 each) • When adding node: steal some partitions from others • Partition boundaries don't change, only assignment to nodes • Used by: Riak, Elasticsearch, CouchBase, Voldemort 2. Dynamic partitioning: • Split partition when it gets too large, merge when too small • Number of partitions adapts to data volume • Used by: HBase, RethinkDB, MongoDB 3. Proportional to nodes: • Fixed number of partitions per node • When new node joins: randomly split existing partitions • Used by: Cassandra ⚠️ Automatic vs manual rebalancing: Kleppmann warns: fully automatic rebalancing can be dangerous. If a node is slow (not dead), automatic rebalancing can cause cascading failures. Many production systems require human confirmation.

sections.heading: Request routing — how clients find the right partition
sections.content:
Three approaches:

1. Client contacts ANY node → node forwards to correct partition
   [Client] → [Node 2] → [Node 5 has partition]

2. Client contacts routing tier (partition-aware load balancer)
   [Client] → [Router] → [Node 5 has partition]

3. Client is partition-aware (knows the mapping)
   [Client] ─────────────→ [Node 5 has partition]
How does anyone know which partition lives where? • ZooKeeper/etcd: centralized metadata store (used by HBase, Kafka, SolrCloud) • Gossip protocol: nodes share partition ownership among themselves (Cassandra) • Config file: manual partition mapping (simple but static) PostgreSQL partitioning (built-in since v10): 
-- Declarative partitioning — no external coordinator needed
CREATE TABLE delivery_events (
    id BIGINT,
    event_time TIMESTAMPTZ,
    event_type TEXT
) PARTITION BY RANGE (event_time);

CREATE TABLE events_2026_q1 PARTITION OF delivery_events
    FOR VALUES FROM ('2026-01-01') TO ('2026-04-01');
CREATE TABLE events_2026_q2 PARTITION OF delivery_events
    FOR VALUES FROM ('2026-04-01') TO ('2026-07-01');
-- Query planner automatically prunes irrelevant partitions
REWE application: • delivery_events: partitioned by month (most queries have date range) • GPS telemetry: partitioned by driver_id hash (even distribution) • Historical orders: partition by year, move old partitions to cold storage

title: Transaction Isolation Deep-Dive — MVCC, Write Skew & Serializability

sections.heading: Beyond the textbook: what DDIA reveals about isolation
sections.content: The standard isolation levels (READ COMMITTED, REPEATABLE READ, SERIALIZABLE) hide enormous complexity. DDIA exposes what really happens: Key insight: "There are big differences in the guarantees that databases actually provide, despite being ostensibly standardized. Nobody really knows what 'repeatable read' means." What each level actually prevents: | Anomaly | Read Committed | Snapshot/RR | Serializable | |---|---|---|---| | Dirty reads | ✓ prevented | ✓ | ✓ | | Dirty writes | ✓ prevented | ✓ | ✓ | | Read skew | ✗ possible | ✓ prevented | ✓ | | Lost updates | ✗ possible | ⚠️ depends on DB | ✓ | | Write skew | ✗ possible | ✗ possible | ✓ prevented | | Phantoms | ✗ possible | ✗ possible | ✓ prevented | ⚠️ MySQL/InnoDB does NOT detect lost updates under repeatable read. PostgreSQL does. This matters in production.

sections.heading: MVCC — how Snapshot Isolation actually works
sections.content:
Multi-Version Concurrency Control — the mechanism behind PostgreSQL's REPEATABLE READ: 
Key principle: readers never block writers, writers never block readers.

Implementation (PostgreSQL):
• Each transaction gets a unique, increasing txid
• Each row has created_by (txid) and deleted_by (txid) fields
• UPDATE = mark old row deleted + insert new row
• Each transaction only sees rows that were:
  1. Created by an already-committed transaction (txid < mine)
  2. NOT deleted, or deleted by a not-yet-committed transaction
Visibility rules (the 4 conditions from DDIA): 1. Ignore writes from transactions still in progress when mine started 2. Ignore writes from aborted transactions 3. Ignore writes from transactions with higher txid (started after me) 4. All other writes are visible 
-- Practical example:
-- TX 12 starts, sees snapshot at that moment
-- TX 13 transfers $100 from account A to B
-- TX 12 still sees the OLD balances (consistent snapshot)
-- Even after TX 13 commits, TX 12's snapshot doesn't change!
Consequences: • Long-running queries see a consistent point-in-time view • VACUUM must clean up old row versions (dead tuples) • Table bloat if VACUUM can't keep up with updates

sections.heading: Write Skew — the anomaly that Snapshot Isolation doesn't prevent
sections.content:
The most dangerous anomaly that many developers don't know about: 
-- Hospital on-call example (from DDIA):
-- Rule: at least 1 doctor must be on-call at all times
-- Currently: Alice and Bob are both on-call

-- TX1 (Alice): "Am I the only one on-call? No, Bob is too. I can leave."
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM doctors WHERE on_call = true AND shift_id = 123;
-- Returns 2 → safe to remove myself
UPDATE doctors SET on_call = false WHERE id = 'alice' AND shift_id = 123;

-- TX2 (Bob): runs the SAME logic at the same time
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM doctors WHERE on_call = true AND shift_id = 123;
-- Also returns 2 (snapshot!) → safe to remove myself
UPDATE doctors SET on_call = false WHERE id = 'bob' AND shift_id = 123;

-- BOTH commit successfully. Now ZERO doctors are on-call! 💀
Pattern: 1. SELECT checks a condition 2. Based on result, application decides to proceed 3. UPDATE/INSERT changes something that would invalidate the condition 4. Another transaction does the same on a DIFFERENT row More examples of write skew: • Booking a meeting room (double-booking) • Claiming a unique username (two users register same name) • Preventing overdraft (two withdrawals check same balance) • Seat reservation on a flight Why snapshot isolation doesn't help: Each transaction reads a consistent snapshot and modifies DIFFERENT rows. No conflict is detected because they don't write to the same row.

sections.heading: Phantoms — the ghost in the machine
sections.content:
Write skew involves a phantom: a row that one transaction's read would have seen if another transaction's write had happened first. 
-- Phantom example:
-- TX1: SELECT * FROM bookings WHERE room = 'A' AND time = '14:00';
-- Returns 0 rows → room is free → INSERT booking

-- TX2: Same query, same time, also returns 0 → INSERT booking
-- Result: DOUBLE BOOKING (phantom row didn't exist when checked)
The problem: You can't use SELECT FOR UPDATE because there's nothing to lock — the row doesn't exist yet! Solutions: 1. Materializing conflicts: 
-- Create a table of time slots explicitly
CREATE TABLE room_slots (
    room_id INT, time_slot TIMESTAMPTZ, PRIMARY KEY (room_id, time_slot)
);
-- Pre-populate all possible slots
-- Now you CAN lock a row:
SELECT * FROM room_slots
WHERE room_id = 'A' AND time_slot = '14:00'
FOR UPDATE;
-- If another TX tries to lock the same slot → blocks
2. Use SERIALIZABLE isolation: 
SET TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL SERIALIZABLE;
-- PostgreSQL will detect the conflict and abort one transaction
-- Application must retry the aborted transaction
3. Application-level constraints: UNIQUE constraints, advisory locks, explicit coordination.

sections.heading: Serializable Isolation — three implementations
sections.content:
The strongest isolation level. Three approaches exist: 1. Actual Serial Execution (single-threaded) • Execute ALL transactions one at a time, on a single CPU core • Surprisingly viable if: transactions are short, data fits in RAM • Used by: VoltDB, Redis, Datomic • Limitation: throughput limited to one core 2. Two-Phase Locking (2PL) • Locks for reads AND writes. Readers block writers, writers block readers. • "If TX A read something, TX B can't write it (until A commits)" • Prevents ALL anomalies but massively reduces concurrency • Used by: MySQL/InnoDB SERIALIZABLE, SQL Server (partially), DB2 • Problem: deadlocks, performance degradation (up to 100x slower) 
-- 2PL in action:
TX1: SELECT * FROM accounts WHERE id = 1; -- acquires shared lock
TX2: UPDATE accounts SET balance = 900 WHERE id = 1;
     -- BLOCKED until TX1 commits/aborts!
-- Readers block writers → significant performance impact
3. Serializable Snapshot Isolation (SSI) • Optimistic: transactions execute without blocking • At commit time: check if any conflicts occurred, abort if so • Used by: PostgreSQL SERIALIZABLE (since 9.1) • Performance: much better than 2PL (readers don't block writers) • Trade-off: higher abort rate under contention 
-- SSI in PostgreSQL:
BEGIN ISOLATION LEVEL SERIALIZABLE;
  SELECT COUNT(*) FROM doctors WHERE on_call = true AND shift_id = 123;
  -- PostgreSQL tracks that this TX read from the doctors table
  UPDATE doctors SET on_call = false WHERE id = 'alice' AND shift_id = 123;
COMMIT;
-- If another TX modified rows that THIS TX read → one gets aborted
-- Application MUST retry: while (true) { try { doTransaction(); break; } catch (SerializationFailure) { retry; } }

sections.heading: Practical decision guide for interviews
sections.content:
When to use each isolation level: READ COMMITTED (PostgreSQL default): • Standard web CRUD applications • Most REST APIs • When read-modify-write is done with atomic operations (UPDATE ... SET x = x + 1) • 90% of application code lives here REPEATABLE READ / Snapshot Isolation: • Reporting queries that need a consistent point-in-time view • Backups (consistent snapshot) • Analytics queries scanning large datasets • Long-running batch jobs SERIALIZABLE: • Financial transactions where correctness > throughput • Booking systems (prevent double-booking) • Any write skew scenario the application can't handle • Always build retry logic when using SERIALIZABLE Application-level alternatives to SERIALIZABLE: 
-- 1. Explicit locking (FOR UPDATE)
SELECT * FROM inventory WHERE product_id = 42 FOR UPDATE;
-- Locks this row, other TXs wait

-- 2. Atomic operations (no read-modify-write cycle)
UPDATE inventory SET quantity = quantity - 1
WHERE product_id = 42 AND quantity > 0;
-- Single statement = atomic, no race condition

-- 3. UNIQUE constraints (prevent duplicates)
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx_booking_unique
  ON bookings (room_id, time_slot);
-- Database enforces uniqueness atomically
REWE delivery system example: • Assigning driver to delivery: use SELECT FOR UPDATE on the driver row • Confirming delivery: atomic UPDATE with WHERE status = 'IN_TRANSIT' • Generating daily reports: REPEATABLE READ to get consistent snapshot • Financial reconciliation: SERIALIZABLE (if possible) or explicit locking
